title: fast-xml-parser Configuration, Entity Limits, Depth, and Dangerous Keys · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss TypeScript decisions for fast-xml-parser configuration, entity limits, depth, and dangerous keys and accepts only runtime and compiler evidence, explicit non-claims, and named browser, Node, network, XML, PostgreSQL, handle, load, accessibility, deployment, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for hardened bounded XML parse adapter. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case…
objectives: Enforce wire, decoded, character, entity, expansion, nesting, attribute, item, text, and duration budgets before and during parsing.
Configure namespaces, attributes, text nodes, value coercion, arrays, order, entities, dangerous properties, allowed tags, and validation deliberately.
Reject or tightly bound DOCTYPE and custom entities using current max-count, depth, total-expansion, expanded-length, and external-reference policy.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

options.text: Run the boundary case, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the result because visible output is the highest authority. That claim does not survive the diagnose the hidden failure evidence boundary.

For diagnose the hidden failure, cast one convenient XML object as a feed, omit reader and publisher outcomes, source and emitted revision, discovery and SSRF scope, Atom and namespace cases, entity and dangerous-key limits, unknown runtime validation, Fetch stream and AbortSignal ownership, PostgreSQL client, Promise settlement, tenant, accessibility, recovery, and controlled Node transfer, then accept plausible objects as feed-service proof.
